data.title: Nebius: Minutes Of Our Call With The Company
data.description:
This is our fifth article on Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS).

In the first article, Nebius Group: Well-Positioned To Be An AI Megatrend Winner, we were among the first to introduce the Nebius opportunity to Seeking Alpha readers. We summarized the core Nebius thesis:

  • Most money is made in global megatrends.
  • AI is the next global megatrend, potentially the biggest value-creation opportunity in history.
  • We are very early in the AI investment cycle.
  • When selecting megatrend winners, focus on founders who have built multi-billion-dollar businesses.
  • Nebius fits all the above criteria and has unique engineering strengths that give the company an unmatched technological advantage, making it one of the AI Megatrend winners.

In the three follow-up articles on Nebius on SA, we focused on commenting on different achievements and valuation updates.

Nebius is a much better company than CoreWeave

We maintain our view that Nebius is very cheap vs CoreWeave, despite Nebius being a much better company, namely:

  1. CoreWeave is a pure AI infrastructure play that provides GPUs. We view Nebius as a whole AI Ecosystem play. Nebius is already involved in different aspects of AI and is entering new AI exposures.
  2. CoreWeave revenues are purely from GPU rentals. Nebius provides additional software and services to its clients. That strategy is more profitable and increases customer stickiness with Nebius.
  3. CoreWeave is highly leveraged, while Nebius has high net cash holdings. CoreWeave's high leverage may impede its future growth.
  4. CoreWeave has a very high client concentration. Nebius does not have that high client concentration risk.
  5. Nebius has a strong management team that has already demonstrated its ability to build international businesses and succeed. They built Yandex, which has beaten Google in the whole Russian-speaking world. Yandex's pre-war valuation exceeded $31 billion. CoreWeave was set up by former crypto traders.
  6. Nebius has over 800 engineers that it took over from Yandex. Some of them working in data centers for almost three decades. CoreWeave does not have such intellectual power.

Despite the above, Nebius trades at a 60% discount compared to CoreWeave. Please see our previous article for details.

Call with Nebius

Last week, we had an opportunity to talk to Nebius. We spoke to the IR team and a technician. The call brought several interesting pieces of information. Below is our summary of the call.

Fit Investment Ideas: How is the current GPU market? Is it oversupplied?

Nebius: Based on our conversations with customers, we have observed a significant demand.

Microsoft's comments were related to their negotiations with OpenAI.

Our customer base is in strong demand. Those customers are utilizing our full stack, and we are providing them with significant additional value beyond the GPU.

In March, we were fully sold out, and we got additional GPUs and are selling the additional capacity well.

We feel very good about the demand for our services.

FII: GPUs payback period

NBIS: On the last call, we discussed the payback period on our investments in GPUs. Regarding payback, we are referring only to GPU data storage revenues.

Our payback estimate does not include revenues for the additional services. Software and services bring our payback even lower. As these revenue streams increase in the future, the payback period should decrease further.

FII: Revenue composition

NBIS: Today, the additional services account for only 10% of GPU revenues.

We are offering part of the additional services for free to attract people, and, for example, giving the software for free to lock customers in.

Revenues from additional services will be growing in the future.

In the long term, we aim to achieve 30% EBIT margins like AWS.

FII: Nebius focuses on the whole AI ecosystem

NBIS: From day one, Nebius was focusing on the whole AI ecosystem.

We are already adding additional services. We do not want to sell only the GPU capacity.

We launched AI Studio and are monetising it well.

We aim to develop a superior AI cloud for our customers, which presents a significant opportunity.

We want to build a whole AI stack.

We are trying to determine what products they will need next and deliver them to our customers.

FII: GPU portfolio

NBIS: As stated on the Q4 call, most of our GPUs are H200.

We had been experiencing some pricing pressure on H100, but since DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), we have seen stability in pricing. Once Blackwell comes, there may be pressure on the pricing of older models.

We have not finalised the pricing on Blackwell.

We are utilising older GPUs for Inference, which is a good and profitable use for them.

FII: Revenue growth

NBIS: We feel comfortable with our revenue guidance. Demand was very good in Q1, and we had many promising discussions that may generate further revenues. We feel very comfortable with where we are today.

Today, we focus on AI core customers – a massive opportunity.

There is also a significant demand from larger customers for longer-term contracts. If a customer comes to us with a large contract, we are prepared to scale up, for example, in our New Jersey facility. We would finance the expansion with asset-backed financing.

Next year, capacity could be significantly increased if we secure a large customer for a longer-term contract.

FII: The data center model

NBIS: DataOne is building the New Jersey center, and we will lease it. The design will be according to our specifications. It is built to our specifications, as is the data center in Finland.

FII: Avride

NBIS: There is a lot of excitement; we are one of the last independent AV companies.

We have great partnerships, and the team is creating a special platform.

It is a capital-intensive business; we seek a partner to fund it. We aim to participate in the upside in the short to medium term.

In the long term, we could see Avride as a source of capital for AI expansion.

We are talking to a lot of potential partners.

FII: Research coverage

NBIS: We are talking to many brokers about the initiation of broker research coverage.

Brokers have been very busy with the CoreWeave IPO. I think they will want first to release the CoreWeave research and then focus on us.

Our takeaways from the call

The company reconfirmed management's statements on the Q4 call and reiterated that demand for its services is strong, which reconfirmed our confidence in the revenue guidance.

A very interesting aspect was the discussion on the research coverage. The CoreWeave IPO was underwritten by a syndicate of 18 banks, led by Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan, and Goldman Sachs. Commonly, most syndicate members produce research coverage. The brokers are now in a 30-day blackout period. It means that the first research reports should be published after May 1, 2025. That is only 14 days from now. Most brokers are now working on CoreWeave initiation reports. If all 18 brokers were to publish, that would provide a lot of education to investors on the sector. This should broaden the investor universe for the industry. That should be bullish for CoreWeave and Nebius.

After the brokers have completed their CoreWeave initiations, they should focus on Nebius. Analysts may want to use their current sector knowledge to quickly complete their Nebius research reports, which could mean we may see an increase in Nebius's initiations in May.

Conclusion

We are very bullish on AI. We believe AI is the next megatrend where most money will be made. Nebius is well-positioned to be one of the AI megatrend winners.

The research reports on CoreWeave, which should arrive in 14 days, could be a major catalyst for CoreWeave and Nebius's rerating.

Subsequent Nebius analyst research initiations should be a further bullish catalyst for Nebius.

data.title: Global Leading Indicators, March 2025 - The World Before Tariffs, And After
data.description:
The March 2025 edition of the global LEI chartbook can be found here. Additional details on the methodology are available here. I’ve added a few new elements: a chart showing the G20 LEI and its three-year rolling Z-score; a comparison between headline LEI diffusion and global equities; and a chart of the first three principal components of the LEIs. Of these, the first component is the most significant - as I’ll explain below.

As the name suggests, leading indicators are designed to provide early signals on the business cycle, and by extension, on the cyclical component in financial markets and the most cyclical individual sectors. However, there are times when turning points or events disrupt the underlying conditions so abruptly that they effectively reset the clock. Trump’s tariff shock - and its implications for global goods and capital mobility - is one such event. But for the record: what did the global economy look like on the eve of this tariff shock? As it turns out, it was doing quite well.

  • As of March, 10 of the 16 leading indicators in this sample were turning up, consistent with a broad-based cyclical upturn. This matches the number from February, post-revisions. That said, it’s a decline from the recent peak of 12 in October last year and represents a broader softening trend by mid-2024. In other words, while global LEIs were still broadly rising at the end of Q1, momentum had begun to fade. We should now prepare for a sharp downturn. The coincident indicator, in this context, is hopelessly behind the curve - it will show strength in Q1 but deteriorate sharply in Q2, with that weakness likely extending into Q3.

  • A significant hit to global leading indicators is now a fair bet - even assuming a relatively swift de-escalation between Trump and China. The key distinction between a short, sharp shock and a more prolonged decline (potentially spiraling into a global recession or financial crisis) hinges on the unpredictable behavior of the U.S. president, his advisors, and possibly Congress - who may yet intervene.

  • The sharp drop in equities so far in April suggests that leading indicators are starting to roll over. Still, it’s always risky to rely solely on market indicators. At this point, it's the combination of deteriorating markets, the real economic impact of U.S. trade policy, and the profound uncertainty injected into the global economy that will drive the deterioration in economic leading indicators.

  • The March LEIs indicate a slight broadening in weakness. Four economies - UK, India, Indonesia, and Brazil - joined the category of “high but falling.” Mexico and Japan remained in outright downturns. We should expect this breadth to deteriorate meaningfully in the months ahead.

  • As for the principal components analysis: this feature is still evolving. The first, and most significant, principal component (PC1) tends to peak during downturns, suggesting that when things fall apart, business cycles become highly correlated. At the end of Q1, PC1 remained low, signaling weak inter-correlation among LEIs - consistent with, though not confirming, a broad-based upturn in the global economy. As a synchronous global downturn begins, we can expect PC1 to rise sharply, though time will tell how strong this shift will be on this occasion. A natural extension of this framework would be to model each LEI using PC1 as an explanatory variable, extracting the residuals to isolate the domestic cycle. Such an approach might yield useful insights for evaluating domestically oriented equity and credit markets in some countries. Stay tuned for more on this.

... And after Trump’s tariffs?

Markets reacted sharply to President Trump’s tariff announcements. Then came the delay on non-China tariffs, followed by weekend news that consumer electronics would be exempt from reciprocal tariffs - effectively lowering the average tariff rate on Chinese imports. Equities are likely to rally on Monday, the dollar to strengthen, and yields to fall - at least at the open. Still, I remain deeply concerned about current market sentiment and the prevailing narratives among analysts. I am hearing three broad narratives at the moment.

The “4D Chess” Theory - Some still believe Trump is executing a master plan to bring China (and the rest of the world) to heel. He’s not. He doesn’t know what he’s doing.

The “Trump Will Blink” Argument - Others believe he’ll ultimately walk back the tariffs. This view gained traction after recent U-turns. But it’s a fragile position. On Friday, stocks didn’t plunge - despite bond market warnings - because investors were waiting for another tweet to send the market soaring. That’s telling. Investors were more afraid of missing a counter-rally than concerned about a potential collapse of the global economy. Betting your investment strategy on a gradual White House retreat? Are you sure Trump won’t interpret a market rally as permission to double down?

Crisis risks are rising, but the Fed won’t allow it to happen - Risks of a global economic and financial crisis are rising, but not to worry, the Fed won’t allow it to happen. Howard Marks - an investor and market observer I have enormous respect for - recently re-posted his “crisis memo”. Mr. Marks finishes with the line that;

Everyone was happy to buy 18-24-36 months ago, when the horizon was cloudless and asset prices were sky-high. Now, with heretofore unimaginable risks on the table and priced in, it’s appropriate to sniff around for bargains: the babies that are being thrown out with the bath water. We’re on the case.

The problem? We haven’t priced in anything close to a 2008-style crisis - let alone the fundamental restructuring of global trade and capital flows implied by Trump’s tariffs. Global equities are down about 20% from their highs, just entering bear market territory. That decline is entirely reasonable if Trump is even half-serious about implementing these tariffs. Last week’s bond market freakout? I suspect much of it was performative - intended to pressure Trump into backing down again. Yes, the breakdown in correlation between the dollar and yields was ugly. But given the economic spectacle playing out, it’s hard to argue that yields falling represents something "breaking," except of course for investor confidence in USD assets commensurate with the stated economic policy of the White House. You think the Fed will step in? Fair enough, but will it be before or after Mr. Trump has fired Jerome Powell? I know that I am just an economist, but if veteran traders and strategists interpret last week’s action as capitulation in the face of sustained U.S.-China tension over trade and capital flows, I’ve got a bridge to sell them. What if this was the plan all along, as Michael Pettis - whose intellectual framework undergirds the disastrous trade policy by the White House - suggests. Jens Nordvig is making a similar point here.

Markets are pricing in a U-turn

It is possible that Mr. Trump is now staging an embarrassing climbdown from a monumental unforced policy error. If he is, it makes sense to buy risk assets. But the question investors need to ask themselves is what price they’re willing to buy, given the tail risk. How do you price in a tail risk that is, almost literally, "unimaginably" bad. The answer, I suspect, is not at all, and then suddenly. If that happens, maybe we’ll need to revisit Howard Marks’ crisis memo. But until then, let’s be honest about what we’re seeing: a market priced for Trump to fold like a cheap suit. The sell-off in Treasuries is linked to that story, based on the idea that it is exactly such price action that will ultimately force the president to fold. And while that outcome is certainly possible - maybe even likely - my gut says this isn’t over just yet.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Moody's Earnings Preview: A Strong Quarter Or Just A Temporary Boost?
data.description:
Introduction

Raise your hand if you are still concerned about the threat of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK).

To many of us, it probably seems ages away. And yet, just three months ago, we were discussing it, and we were hearing that American AI companies were in trouble.

It seems we are in a whole different world now, where the main concern is tariffs. Who knows what the future will bring? Perhaps this will be a long-lasting issue. Perhaps, three months from now, we will be talking about something unthinkable right now.

In January, I covered Moody's Corporation (NYSE:MCO). I suggested that the plan of the U.S. administration to invest up to $500B in AI infrastructure through 2029 would lead to a massive new issuance of debt. So, I argued that a business such as Moody's that hinges on credit ratings could easily benefit from this massive investment cycle.

That wasn't the first time I dealt with Moody's. I became interested in it as I scanned Berkshire's portfolio and attempted to reverse-engineer the rationale that led Warren Buffett to select Moody's as one of Berkshire's top holdings. Moody's wide moat, strong pricing power, and its profile as a global risk assessment powerhouse make it a true cornerstone of many portfolios. Therefore, it usually trades at a premium, with a 30x earnings multiple. As a result, it is difficult to find a good buying opportunity. The last one was in 2022, when debt issuance sharply decreased as a consequence of higher interest rates.

However, even those not interested in becoming a shareholder should keep an eye on Moody's earnings because the company is deeply rooted in today's economy and can therefore provide us with insightful data on the direction the economy is going.

Now, Moody's divides its operations into two main segments, namely, Moody's Investors Service (MIS) and Moody's Analytics (MA). As Seeking Alpha's analyst RB Equity has recently shown, Moody's is more concentrated than its main peer, S&P Global (SPGI). This makes Moody's margins a bit higher than S&P Global's, since the most profitable business is credit rating.

As the new earnings season opens, I believe Moody's earnings will deserve our time. In fact, they should somewhat recall how the world seems to have changed in a few months. This should translate into a relatively easy Q1 forecast, and into an outlook on Q2 and the rest of the year more opaque and difficult to foretell.

Moody's Q1 Earnings Preview

To understand what the upcoming earnings could look like, we should start from the last available financial data. Moody's delivered strong FY2024 results, with a 20% revenue growth to $7.1B, an adj. operating margin of 48.1% (+420bps), and a strong 26% EPS increase to $12.47. MIS reported revenue growth of 33%, showing that 2024 was a favorable year for credit ratings, given the easing policy of many central banks. The adj. op. margin of this division was 60.1%, which is truly outstanding. MA reported annualized recurring revenue of $3.3B and a full-year revenue growth of 8%, with an adjusted operating margin of 30.7%.

Moody's 2025 outlook projected an adj. operating margin of 50% and FY EPS between $14.00 and $14.50.

Surely, this was a strong year, and the company issued a FY 2025 guidance that expects its growth path to keep moving steadily in the right direction. Total revenues are expected to increase in the high single-digit range, with an operating margin of approximately 50%. What stands out is the steady and continuously growing FCF generation, which should be around $2.5B by the end of this year.

If we break down Moody's guidance by its two main segments, we have the following forecasts:

  • MIS sees issuance growth in the low-single-digit range, but the adj. op margins should be between 62% and 63%.
  • MA sees revenue growth in the high-single-digit range, an ARR growth of around 10%, and an adj. operating margin between 32% and 33%.

Now, we should understand why Moody's forecasts are quite reliable. First of all, its analytics business has an extremely strong customer retention of 94% (MIS enjoys more or less the same trend). As a result, Moody's can count on a very safe annual recurring revenue growth that is mostly fueled by 7% upgrades and price hikes, coupled with the acquisition of new customers.

Another strength of Moody's is the debt renewal cycle through 2028. When the interest rate increased, many customers decided to postpone their new debt issuance, waiting for the Fed and other central banks to pivot to a more dovish stance. We see that only in the U.S., there will be almost $2.5T of non-financial corporate bonds and loans that will come to maturity in the next four years. This is a massive tailwind for Moody's. If we add in the EMEA area, we have another +$2T that will come to maturity.

During the last earnings call, however, we learned that debt issuance grew in 2024 and is expected to grow this year as well:

We rated nearly 6.2 trillion of issuance in 2024. That’s an increase of 42% compared to 2023. [...] Throughout last year, our Ratings teams issued a press release related to a credit opinion on average every 20 minutes. And that’s without needing to meaningfully increase our analytical staffing levels. And very importantly, we did this while maintaining the robust controls that the market and our regulators expect from us.

This also shows us how Moody's runs a business that is highly sensitive to the positive contribution of AI, which can make it increase its rating output without needing to hire more workers.

As a matter of fact, AI is only considered the fifth positive current benefit that Moody's currently benefits from. The other four are, as Moody's management explained during the earnings call:

  1. The ongoing expansion and evolution of the debt capital markets
  2. The increasing pace of digital transformation and automation across banks and insurers.
  3. The need for businesses to know more about who they’re doing business with.
  4. The need across industries to understand the financial impact of extreme weather events.

Moody's is a FCF-generative powerhouse. After all, its services require very little capex and, therefore, it is usually able to have an FCF to net income conversion rate around, if not above 100%.

As a consequence, Moody's constantly returns cash to its shareholders. This year, at least $2B should be returned via buybacks and dividends.

So, having seen all this, what should we expect from Moody's Q1 earnings? If we look at the current consensus, we see that analysts truly expect the company to follow a regular growing pattern.

Well, the new variable is certainly tariffs. But I argue that it is not only about tariffs. As Aswath Damodaran has recently shown, the market is starting to increase the price of risk to over 5%, suggesting that geopolitical instability, coupled with the unpredictability of some choices the U.S. administration could take, is making investors demand a higher equity risk premium.

This could affect Moody's earnings in two ways. We could actually see a stronger Q1 than expected. In fact, many importers who hurried to bring goods into the U.S. before the tariffs were announced, we could see that many corporations quickly took on new debt in anticipation of possibly higher rates or higher risk premiums that the market will offer. After all, tariffs increase the likelihood of a surge in inflation.

So, I would not be surprised to see MIS report a strong Q1 that could beat expectations. However, it could be thanks to a pull-forward effect. So, unless during the earnings call we learn something different, I would still be cautious about this educated guess I explained.

On the other hand, most of our attention will be paid to what Moody's expects after the Trump administration announced the import tariffs.

Currently, analysts expect Moody's to report $1.9B in revenues, which would be a 5.6% YoY growth. I believe Moody's could come close to $2B if some customers anticipate their debt issuance in anticipation of deteriorating economic conditions. But, as I said, this is a big "if" that will need to be proven by the company itself. With a net income margin of around 30%, we could have Moody's bottom line be around $600M. This gives us EPS of $3.33, which is a bit below the current estimate of $3.55. But this still doesn't factor in two things: margin improvement and share buybacks.

Moody's buys back 1M to 2M shares per quarter, so the share count at the end of Q2 could be around 178.5 M. With a margin improvement of 50 bps, we could have net income of $610M, which, divided by 178.5M shares, gives us EPS of $3.42.

So, I think the current consensus is a bit optimistic. We could see an earnings surprise, but Moody's doesn't always beat expectations, as we can see below.

Sometimes, analysts are too optimistic, and even a company with the qualities Moody's has fails to meet these forecasts.

If Moody's reports earnings of around $3.40, it is perfectly aligned to close the fiscal year within its EPS guidance above $14.00, so I would not be troubled.

Valuation

What is more difficult compared to what we have done so far is to determine whether the stock is a buy before earnings or not.

A few days ago, the stock fell to $400, after having reached an ATH close of $530 in February. However, this has simply placed Moody's fwd earnings multiple around 34. The fwd EV/EBITDA is also steep: 22.5. The fwd P/FCF is 28.5. We should cross-read these multiples through the growth assumptions baked into them. Moody's PEG is 2, which suggests that the stock is trading at twice the rate of its expected EPS growth. As a matter of fact, the average EPS growth Moody's reports is around 15%. However, context does matter, and Moody's trades at these multiples because of the strong pricing power and the moat it enjoys.

We could look at the FCF yield, which is currently 3.2%. This can make Moody's appear a bit more affordable. But the dividend yield is below 1%, and the 5-yr yield on cost is only 1.5%. Yes, the company's FCF grows at 10% per year, and its dividend growth is more or less the same.

Although Moody's is exceptional, it is hard for me to argue that right now it is a steal and that we should buy it hand over fist. Perhaps, a good strategy could be to build an initial position and then increase it by dollar cost averaging. But we have to be aware that, at these valuations, we have little margin of safety. Moreover, S&P Global trades at a valuation which is a bit more attractive (fwd PE 33; fwd PEG 1.9; fwd EV/EBITDA 21.8; P/FCF 26), so I would rather turn to this stock if I were to buy a rating agency right now. Therefore, I consider Moody's a hold right now.

data.title: American Express: Premium Growth Underappreciated
data.description:
Thesis Summary

I initiate coverage on American Express (NYSE:AXP) with a Buy rating and $289 PT. American Express is a leading global provider of premium charge and credit card products, payment solutions and travel related services. My Buy thesis is based on an expectation for a reacceleration in net income growth to +11.0% in CY25E, supported by continued strength in premium card fee growth and consumer spending coupled with accelerating international penetration (particularly among younger affluent Millennials and Gen Z).

I believe the Street underappreciates AXP's closed-loop model, premium customer base and earnings visibility. I apply an 18.0x forward P/E multiple, which is consistent with its historical median and represents an appropriate premium to the consumer finance peer group in my view, given superior earnings visibility and structural attributes. Key risks include potential regulatory changes and macro sensitivity to consumer spending and credit, but I view these as manageable given conservative underwriting (Net write-offs 2.7%) and diversified revenue streams. Overall, I see risk/reward as attractive at current levels, and I'm comfortable with my Buy rating and $289 PT.

Above Consensus on NI

I forecast Amex CY25E net income growth of 11.0%, balancing strong topline drivers with continued headwinds. I am above consensus on NI, reflecting:

1) Mid-to-high teens growth in net card fees, driven by continued focus on premium cards and ongoing international expansion.

2) 8-10% revenue growth, reflecting resilient consumer spending and accelerating growth across international markets.

3) Continued rewards rate pressures as competition remains intense.

4) Increasing regulatory headwinds, leading to higher compliance-related expenses and placing pressure on growth and margins.

I note management continues to highlight confidence around delivering EPS growth within the 12-16% range, though acknowledge that share repurchases will benefit EPS growth over net income.

Operating Leverage

Additionally, I note the implied low-single-digit growth in operating expenses reflects some operating leverage, but also acknowledges variable customer engagement expenses will continue to grow faster than revenues, creating a headwind to margins. As such, I forecast 11.0% net income growth, vs. 7.7% consensus, balancing these positives and headwinds.

CY25 CY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $71.40B 18 $77.11B 18
EBITDA $11.24B 18 $12.14B 18
Net Income $10.91B 18 $12.47B 18
EPS $15.24 21 $17.52 21
SG&A Expenses $20.69B 18 $22.35B 18

source: FMP (Wall Street Estimates)

Premium Card Growth

Amex's strategic pivot toward premium card offerings will drive net card fee revenue growth of +16% YoY ($8.4B in FY'24), underpinning my forecasted 11.0% net income growth for CY25E. Premium cards now represent 70% of new accounts, with millennials and Gen Z-60% of new acquisitions-entering peak earning years and demonstrating strong affinity for high-fee, high-benefit products. Recent fee hikes (Delta Reserve: $550→$650, India Platinum: +10%) paired with targeted benefit enhancements (elite hotel memberships, Accor Plus Traveller) reinforce pricing power and customer stickiness, insulating AXP from mid-tier competition (Chase Sapphire Reserve, Capital One Venture X). I see premium fee growth as a key valuation driver and supporting upside to my CY25E NI and PT.

~16% Fee Growth Propels Surge in Net Income

In 2024, Amex will grow net card fee revenue by 16% YoY, to $8.4B from $7.3B in 2023, contributing to 11.0% net income growth in CY25E and accounting for ~35-40% of the gain. 13M new premium cardholders were acquired in 2024, equating to 6.6% YoY growth, of which 72% were from the Millennial and Gen Z cohorts, who boast a 23% higher fee retention rate than other age cohorts. Amex has a 25% market share in fee-based premium cards, among the highest in the industry (vs. Chase Sapphire Reserve's 18% and Venture X's 18%), and the Amex Platinum annual fee is $695 and Gold is $325, a 28-42% premium to alternative offerings.

Premium Edge but Pressured Outlook

While CEO Stephen Squeri noted during the 4Q24 earnings call that Amex's strategy of attracting high spenders in core segments is working and fee income growth is structural, not just cyclical, CFO Christophe Le Caillec showed conviction with a 17% dividend increase. Together, this points to Amex's dominance and sustainability in premium card fee growth, which is an important earnings driver. However, competition from elevated sign-up bonuses by Chase and Capital One and regulatory constraints on fee structures are a legitimate risk to the outlook.

30-50bps NIM Compression

Amex is now facing 30-50bps of NIM compression in FY25 from higher credit loss provisions and macro headwinds, which will make it difficult for the company to achieve its FY25E EPS guidance of $15.00-15.50. AXP reported 18% YoY growth in credit loss provisions in 1Q25 to $1.4bn, and net write-offs increased from 1.9% in 4Q24 to 2.1% in 1Q25, both indicative of deteriorating credit quality.

Lower Revenue Guidance

In addition, revenue growth guidance was lowered to 7.2% (from 8-10% for FY25) due to tariffs and slowing small-business spend, and management is now acknowledging that 35% of 1Q25 volume growth came from millennials with FICO scores <740 in retail categories that are sensitive to tariffs. While Basel III changes added an incremental $2bn of share repurchases, helping EPS, that is not enough to offset the NIM compression and credit risks. Investors must now weigh the potential benefits from American Express's premium customer base against these emerging headwinds that are likely to pressure profitability.

Key Risks to Buy Thesis

1) Credit quality deteriorates amid the macro headwinds, particularly given AXP's rising net write-offs and exposure to millennials with lower FICO scores in tariff-sensitive sectors such as retail and restaurants.

2) Fintech disruption and digitization are accelerating faster than I expect, putting the long-term visibility of AXP's earnings power and closed-loop model at risk.

3) Continued pressure on equity markets due to tariff uncertainty and rising inflation, causing direct multiple compression across the board.

Valuation

I value AXP at 18.0x CY25E PE on $11.2bn net income, to derive my $289 price target. The multiple is in-line with AXP's historical median at 17.9x (47th %tile) and embeds a justified premium to the peer group median of 10.3x (53rd %tile). The 75% premium vs peers can be rationalized by AXP's structurally stronger growth profile in premium card fees and international expansion. Relative to its own history, AXP trades at the 51.9th percentile of its historical range, while relative to the peer group, AXP ranks in the 100th percentile and trades above comparables such as BAC (10.5x), JPM (11.4x) and DFS (9.8x). AXP has historically traded at a premium to this group, but the gap is currently wider than the LTA in several instances. I view my multiple as appropriate given the differentiated closed loop model and earnings visibility, balancing the risk of macro and regulatory headwinds.

data.title: Bears In Control On Wall Street Amid Economic Headwinds
data.description:
Brief/Overview

In my previous article on the S&P 500 index, I cautioned that the valuations and the economy were causing concern. Additionally, I’d flagged the concentration of the top companies within the index, rising insider selling and over-optimism of retail investors with respect to the equity market.

In this article, I’ll be covering more issues such as the impact of tariffs on the economy, widening credit spreads, discretionary spending, and valuations and providing the readers with some technical analysis too.

Thesis/Triggers

Trump's Tariff Tantrum and the R-word

As we all know, on 2nd April 2025, US President Donald Trump announced the tariff rates applied to each country. While it initially seemed to be a bit random, people quickly figured out the formula to calculate the tariff rate: 0.5 x [(US Trade Deficit with Nation A/US Imports from Nation A)]. This received its own criticism, but I will leave that out to focus on the economic impact and the equity markets.

On the face of it, these tariffs are stagflationary, which means they could potentially lead to higher inflation and lower US GDP growth. This hypothesis was also confirmed by Jerome Powell. Various analysts and economists have tried to estimate the impact of the tariffs on the above-mentioned economic metrics.

According to Allianz Global, these tariffs have unleashed a significant headwind of up to 1.3% of the US GDP, potentially reducing growth by 1.3% in the short term (assumed 2025). On a global scale, the impact could be at least a 1% hit to the global GDP and a 0.7% to 1.5% increase in global inflation. If inflation rises globally and there is a hit to the GDP, it will naturally affect the earnings of US companies, as people will spend less, be it buying iPhones or Tesla cars or selling or marketing their products on Google, Meta, or Amazon. It is, in fact, the tech sector or companies in the Nasdaq 100 (NASDAQ:QQQ) that face the most risk from a global slowdown, as more than half their revenues come from outside the United States, based on research from JPM AM as shown in their Guide to Markets for Q2 2025.

JP Morgan is the first of the major Wall Street banks to warn of a recession. The firm’s chief economist, Michael Feroli, informed clients that the bank expects US GDP to contract by (-) 0.3%, revised significantly lower from previous expectations of 1.3% growth, meaning a 1.6% growth headwind to US GDP. Feroli further added that the expectation is of a second quarter recession with GDP growth estimated to be -1% in Q3 and -0.5% in Q4. Additionally, Feroli mentioned that they expect the unemployment rate to rise to 5.3%, and as one would think, he too opined that the tariffs would bite corporate margins. Consumer demand would also be faced with the inflation obstacle, as nominal wage growth has moderated significantly lately.

While JPM believes we will get a second half recession, the Atlanta Fed’s GDP-Now tool is already expecting a -2.8% growth (rather de-growth) in Q1 in the calendar year 2025. Even if one were to adjust for the gold imports, then GDP is still expected to shrink 0.8%.

What I think everyone else might be missing out on is the second-order impacts of everything I’ve covered. Not only will tariffs hurt consumption and increase inflation, but the increased economic and policy uncertainty they will create will also hinder investment and capital expenditure by corporates, especially if they start to see a deceleration in their growth. Now, this also has multiplier effects throughout the economy. All in all, I feel that recession could come faster than expected and could be deeper than most expect, as the Fed will also be faced with tariff-fuelled inflation, in addition to Trump’s supposed unwillingness to expand government (or willingness to shrink government spending).

Though the news just came in that Trump has put a 90-day tariff pause and lowered the tariff to 10% for these 90 days on all countries except China, and markets absolutely flew, I do not think this makes a significant difference to my bearish thesis on stocks. Even a 10% tariff is substantial and will have a large impact on trade and negative impacts on consumption and future fixed capital formation owing to the prevailing uncertainty around current policy and future economic conditions.

Tighter Financial Conditions and Wider Credit Spreads

Another catalyst impacting the equity market is the credit default spreads. Wider credit spreads are one of many signs of tighter financial conditions. Wider credit spreads also make corporate borrowing more expensive and thereby more difficult, or rather less financially viable. This has a negative impact on the amount of capital investment in the economy. This is usually considered a sign that the economy is weakening and is a sign of increased risks in the financial system.

In the last few weeks, credit spreads across all credit ratings have risen sharply. Most notably, the junk debt spreads have risen sharply in the last week. Currently at 292 bps, the spread is the highest in nearly 18 months (see image below).

The below image shows the relation between the spread of the BB-rated corporate bonds’ yield to the benchmark 10-year treasury bond yield on the RHS and the annual change of the S&P 500 on the LHS. It seems very clear that we were at historically low levels a couple of months ago, and now as the spread widens, investors may want to reduce their exposure to risky assets such as equities. Another pattern that I am able to observe is that as the spread widens up to 4%, the annual change in the S&P 500 starts to turn negative. The spread has risen 88 basis points in the last few weeks with little sign of reversing so far.

A significant increase in the high-yield spread can be one of the earliest indicators of an impending bear market, as it reflects credit markets beginning to price in higher risks. Wider high-yield spreads typically have the following impact:

Companies may struggle to finance their debt at lower costs, and this may reduce profitability or lower capital expenditure in the company, as many projects may become financially infeasible (NPV negative) at higher discounting rates. A widening spread often reflects concerns that the economy is heading for a slowdown, which further reinforces a lower willingness for businesses to invest in the near term, lower job creation and reduced consumer spending.

I believe wider credit spreads will have the highest impact on the Russell 2000 index compared to the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq 100. This is because the average rating of the Russell 2000 Fixed Income Index, which comprises debt of public companies tracked by Russell 2000, is BB-/B+. Thus, small-cap companies in the Russell 2000 will struggle the most in raising funds for capital expenditure or even working capital, and these loans will become more expensive. As per the JP Morgan Guide to Markets for Q2, the EPS of the small companies is expected to grow 38-44% after showing de-growth of 12% and 17% in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Herein lies the maximum scope for a downward revision of earnings with no clear growth driver in the economy, 40% of the small cap companies being unprofitable already and widening credit spreads pushing borrowing costs higher.

While it is not a forecaster of recessions and there is no clearly defined level above which a recession can be forecasted, the sharp rise in credit spreads is unlikely to reverse any time soon, in my opinion. This is because government spending cuts, a higher rate of unemployment, and expectations that inflation would reverse high due to tariffs and other things all increase the uncertainty around future GDP growth, resulting in rising corporate bond yields, not just yields of government bonds.

I would recommend closely watching high-yield spreads and adjusting their portfolio accordingly. The Nasdaq’s own website suggests the current situation (during December 2024) of tight credit spreads resembles the economic prosperity of the 1990s. With yields rising sharply in the last few weeks, one should wonder if the phase of prosperity is ending and something like the Dot-com bubble burst could follow, perhaps in AI and semiconductor-related stocks in the near future.

Discretionary spending on the decline

Alongside the headwinds from tariffs, another headwind to US GDP growth is Americans cutting back on discretionary spending. As of Q4 2024, private final consumption expenditure was 68.1% of the US GDP, up from 67.7% in Q4 2023. As we head into Q2 2025, there are increasing reports that consumers are already cutting back on discretionary spending or certainly plan to do so over the coming months.

A McKinsey research article shows that US consumers are looking to cut back on discretionary spending. The below image shows various product categories or sectors under the discretionary spending umbrella. As can be seen, all these categories show that, on a net basis, people were looking to cut their discretionary spending. In some categories, the number of people cutting their spending decreased, meaning fewer people were cutting spending; however, when netted, of those looking to increase spending, we still can see that the number of people pulling back on spending exceeds those looking to spend more.

The Conference Board’s data showed a similar trend as the closely tracked Consumer Confidence dropped to a multi-year low of 92.9. It was primarily the Expectations Index-based short-term outlook for income, business, and labour market conditions of consumers’ that led the decline in the consumers’ confidence, as it dropped 9.6 points to a 12-year low of 65.2 and way below the threshold of 80 that usually signals a recession ahead as per the Conference Board.

Wells Fargo’s annual Money Study found that 76% of Americans are looking to cut back on spending, higher than the 67% recorded in 2024. The younger demographic seemed more under financial duress, with 79% of millennials and 82% of Gen Z surveyed looking to cut back on their spending. The places where people are cutting back on spending are travel plans (74%), home renovations (39%), relocating (30%), and buying a home (30%). Lower sales of discretionary items can lead to worsening economic contractions.

Earnings and Valuations

The S&P 500 seems highly overvalued based on various valuation metrics, such as the P/E Ratio, the Buffett Indicator, among others.

First up is the Shiller 10-year P/E ratio, or the Cyclically Adjusted P/E Ratio, which currently stands at 34.09 as of the market close on 9th April 2025. Data from CMV shows that this 34.09 is nearly 2 standard deviations above the modern era market average of ~20.4. This has only happened twice before, during the DotCom bubble and the post-covid 2021 market melt-up. So, it is very reasonable to say that the possibility of a bear market beginning here is much higher than a new bull market starting here. Even if there may not be a price correction, there would need to be a time-wise correction with growing earnings for the P/E ratio to revert towards the mean.

Morgan Stanley's Mike Wilson and Citi's Head of US Equity Strategy, Scott Chronert, have already lowered their S&P 500 2025 EPS forecasts to $257 and $255, respectively. If I were to put an 18x multiple on these forward earnings, the price target on the S&P 500 would be about 4600, nearly 15% below current levels.

Research from Sherwood (see image below), using data from Bloomberg, shows that on a net basis more companies in the S&P 500 are showing downward revisions of earnings estimates than positive. This kind of repricing risk is yet to be factored in by the markets, and I am of the opinion that the upcoming earnings season will force more downgrades than upgrades.

The next valuation indicator is the Buffett Indicator, which is a ratio of the total market capitalisation to the GDP of the country. For the United States, the Buffett Indicator stands at close to 200%. This is close to 2 standard deviations above the historical trend. The other times that this has happened were during the DotCom bubble and the Nifty50 bubble. What followed in the preceding decade has been a lost decade for stocks. Based on this, I would not be surprised if we got another decade of near-zero or low single-digit returns. The exponential trendline (available on the linked site) suggests the market would be fairly valued at 126% of GDP, suggesting the broader market or the stock market as a whole would need to decrease by 35-40% (37% to be precise) to be considered fairly valued. That would mean the S&P 500 would need to be in the mid-3300s (close to the 2022 lows), the Nasdaq 100 would be at 11600-11700 (or the QQQ ETF at ~280), and the Russell 2000 Small-Cap Index would be at ~1160 (or ~115 for the IWM ETF), or around 25000 on the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Obviously, not all stocks or not all indices will decline equally; some would be more vulnerable than others, and at the centre of that, in my opinion, would be high-flying tech stocks, which I hope to cover in the coming weeks as they announce their earnings. Notably, the Mag7 stocks make up close to a third of the S&P 500 index, and the NASDAQ 100 is full of technology and related stocks. So those would be more vulnerable and will probably decline more than the Dow Jones or the Russell 2000.

While we covered the S&P 500 and the total market above, I will now shed some light upon the valuations of the high-flying Nasdaq 100 index.

Based on data from S&P Capital IQ, the Nasdaq 100 trades at a forward P/E of 24.55x. This puts it in the 78th percentile in the last 20 years, compared to a median NTM P/E of 20.73x and an average NTM P/E of 21.10x. This means that there is scope for a 15% drawdown before the index is at its average valuation multiple. However, the silent risk lies in the form of downward earnings revisions. As highlighted earlier, the momentum of earnings revisions is strongly in favour of the bears, and the tech sector could be worst hit by a global economic slowdown, as 56% of its revenues come from outside the USA.

Technical Analysis

Apart from fundamental factors, we have evidence from the price action on the charts to suggest that a bear market here is more likely than a fresh bull market.

S&P-500 index (SPX) (NYSEARCA:SPY)

First up, I have the monthly charts of the S&P 500 index. The below image shows a rising wedge being formed on the S&P 500 monthly chart. The green line marks two previous tops – the May 2007 high before the 2008 financial crisis and the December 2021 before the 2022 rate-hiking cycle bear market. The red lines mark the bottoms – March 2009 during the financial crisis, the March 2020 lows and then the lows in the October 2022 cycle preceding the AI-led bull market.

Based on the wedge and the Fibonacci retracement (0.5), my intermediate target would be 480 (~12% drawdown) on the SPY around the wedge support within the next 6 months, and if that gets broken, we could get lower levels of 410 (22-23% drawdown) and then 350-ish.

The final chart I am presenting for the S&P 500 is the daily chart with the 50 Day SMA (Orange) at 573.31 and the 200 Day SMA (Purple) at 573.83, which means we have a death cross on the S&P 500. The last time this happened was during the 2022 bear market, with the index declining a further 18-20% after the death cross.

The Nasdaq 100 (QQQ)

The first chart (see image below) is the weekly chart of the QQQ ETF, and the red trend line was the supporting upward trendline, drawn since the lows of 2022. This trendline has supported the ETF at lows such as March 2023 (the small banking crisis), November 2023, early August 2024 (the reverse carry trade flash crash), September 2024, and January 2025 until the trendline finally broke down in February 2025. There is support at the 400 psychological level where the index bottomed recently, which is about 10% below current levels.

The below chart is the monthly chart of the QQQ ETF. I’ve drawn a Fibonacci retracement from the lows of 2022 to the highs of December 2024 (539.15) or February 2025 (540.81), which also indicates a double top. The index has so far bottomed at the 0.5 retracement level, which comes in at about ~$398. That presents the first level of support. The next is the 0.618 retracement at ~$364, and that would be the next level of support for the tech index.

The below chart shows the Death Cross pattern forming on the QQQ ETF as the 50-day SMA (in orange) stands at 491.92, having moved below the 200-day SMA (in purple) at 492.94. The last time this happened was during the 2022 bear market, leading to a drawdown of close to 40%.

All in all, my first target for the index would be near $400 (a 10% drawdown on the QQQ), and then a $360 target (about a 20% drawdown).

The Russell 2000 (NYSEARCA:IWM)

The R2K index, as it is popularly called, has largely failed to participate in the 2023-24 bull market. The red upward trendline offered support to the index at the COVID lows as well as in October 2023. Intra-month, the R2K has broken below the red support trendline, and based on charts of the other timeframes, I would think this is a good time to sell or go short on the Russell 2000 index.

The below image shows a weekly chart of the Russell 2000 index. The index topped out at the green upward resistance trendline in November 2024 and has since broken down below two support trendlines (both in red). The index also re-tested the dotted support/resistance horizontal line after breaking down below it last week. Now that the retest has been confirmed, I would think the index is headed lower.

Things look much worse on the daily chart for the Russell 2000 (image below), as it has broken below a major supporting trendline as well as had a death cross, as the 50-day SMA (orange) has crossed below the 200-day SMA (purple), and another 15-20% decline can be very much expected, in my opinion.

Many may point to the fact that the indices are very close to the 200-Week EMA suggesting a bottom is close. However, the 200-week EMA for both the Nasdaq 100 and the S&P 500 is about 10-12-15% lower from current levels, and I would be happy to revisit the indices once my targets of a 10% drawdown are hit and the indices are closer to the 200W EMA. However, I would also like to remind the readers that the said moving average was of little help during the recessions of the DotCom bubble burst and the 2008 financial crisis. So, if we get a negative GDP print in Q1, I would expect the support of the 200-week EMA to be of little help and would suggest that investors avoid buying dips at that level.

Conclusion

Going forward, and as highlighted in this article, GDP growth, corporate profit growth and, as a consequence, the stock market face several headwinds, such as tariffs creating an environment of economic uncertainty, declining discretionary spending, and wider credit spreads. In light of said factors, I am changing my stance on the equity indices from ‘Hold’ in January 2025 to ‘Sell’.

During the 2022 bear market, all indices fell 15-20% after a death cross occurred, and we also have 0.618 Fibonacci retracement support levels on both indices around the 20% drawdown levels.

data.title: NIO: Trade War Could Be A Major Boon
data.description:
NIO’s (NYSE:NIO) recent earnings were a mixed bag. The company reported over 40% YoY growth in deliveries and more than 400 basis point YoY improvement in gross margin. However, despite this improvement, the company reported an EPS and revenue miss. There has also been a dilution, as the company raised $450 million from a share placement. Since the recent earnings call, the stock has dropped over 30%, which shows the negative sentiment towards the stock. There are a few major tailwinds that should help NIO in the next few quarters. I believe that the recent tariff war between the U.S. and China could improve NIO’s position in the domestic and international markets. According to the CEO, NIO has 40% market share in China’s premium BEV market priced over RMB300,000 or over $41,000. Tesla (TSLA) could face consumer and regulatory backlash in China, which can help NIO in the premium segment. In the previous article, I mentioned that NIO could deliver good deliveries in the earnings call.

NIO is also planning on launching its Firefly model in the European Union soon. The European Commission had imposed tariffs on Chinese EVs last year, but the changing trading dynamics could lead to a better negotiated tariff settlement between China and the European Union. This could help improve NIO’s margin and profitability in the next few quarters. NIO’s losses are the main headwind for the stock sentiment, and any improvement in this metric could deliver a bullish momentum to the stock. The consensus revenue estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $16.39 billion, which gives NIO stock a forward P/S ratio of 0.48, making it quite attractive.

Mixed earnings and price drop

NIO reported deliveries of 72,689 in Q4 2024 compared to 50,045 in Q4 2023. This is over 45% YoY growth, which is quite good when we compare with the 13% YoY decline reported by Tesla in the recent Q1 press release. NIO’s premium cars are competing directly with Tesla in China and giving it good competition. We have seen Tesla reduce prices a number of times in the last few quarters due to higher competition.

Figure: NIO’s deliveries in the last few quarters. Source: Company Filings

Despite the intense competition, NIO has been able to improve the margin metrics. The vehicle margin improved to 13.1% in Q4 2024 from 11.9% in Q4 2023. The gross margin also improved to 11.7% in Q4 2024 from 7.5% in Q4 2023. This is a 420 basis points improvement in one year, which is quite good when we look at the pricing wars within its segment.

Figure: Margin improvement by NIO. Source: Company Filings

At the same time, NIO missed its EPS by $0.11, showing negative EPS of $0.44. The revenue metric was also missed by $52 million.

Figure: EPS and revenue miss by NIO. Source: Seeking Alpha

This changed the sentiment towards the stock, leading to a 30% correction since the earnings. However, despite the EPS and revenue miss, the fundamentals look quite good. The faster delivery growth helps in gaining economies of scale for its battery swap technology. More people using this option will help in achieving faster break-even in this segment.

Trade war could be a major tailwind

Over the last few weeks, the tariff situation has gripped Wall Street. Analysts are still looking at the possible impact on different industries and segments. One likely scenario is that there are higher purchases of domestic brands in China. Tesla and Musk are close to the current White House administration, and we could see a customer backlash against this brand in China. Tesla’s recent earnings showed a revenue of $20.9 billion from China in 2024 compared to $21.7 billion in 2023. This slowdown in sales in China could accelerate significantly due to the tariff wars.

Figure: Tesla’s revenue from different regions. Source: Tesla Filings

NIO’s CEO mentioned that the company has a 40% market share in China’s BEV segment priced over RMB300,000 (approximately $41,000). There is a direct competition between Nio and Tesla in this segment. Any major decline in Tesla’s sales could reduce the pricing pressure from NIO and allow the company to show better deliveries and margins.

Figure: CEO’s comments in the recent earnings. Source: Company Filings

Another likely situation to consider is the changing dynamics between China and the European Union. Due to the higher tariffs from the US, China would need a better trading arrangement with the European Union in order to sell any excess goods. The European Union had earlier announced additional tariffs on Chinese EVs, going as high as 45% in October 2024. Recent announcements show a rapid move towards removing these tariffs and setting an alternative minimum price for cars.

NIO will soon launch its Firefly model in the EU. It faces an additional tariff of 20.7%, bringing the total tariffs to 30.7%. A better trading negotiation between China and the EU can bring down these tariffs and allow NIO to expand in a very important market. A successful launch in the EU could boost the margins for the company and change the forward revenue and EPS projections of the company.

Attractive price after recent dip

According to consensus estimates, NIO is expected to report 38.2% YoY revenue growth in the fiscal year ending Dec 2025 and another 30.6% YoY revenue growth in the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. This is a very strong revenue growth momentum. At the same time, the company has shown a good improvement in gross margin, which should help improve the overall profitability. The stock is currently trading at a P/S ratio of 0.68 for the fiscal year ending Dec 2025 and a 0.48 P/S ratio for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. This is quite attractive pricing for an EV stock, which has a number of positive tailwinds working in its favor.

Figure: Forward revenue estimates of NIO. Source: Seeking Alpha

A big factor in the future stock momentum is the ability of the management to deliver on its promised cost cuts and operating efficiency. NIO’s gross margin is still behind market leader, BYD. As mentioned above, if we see a lower competitive environment in the premium BEV segment in China, it could help NIO improve its margins. Entry into lucrative international markets like the EU should also boost the margin growth potential.

Despite some negative sentiments towards NIO, I believe the company is in a good position due to changing trading dynamics and a better competitive environment in China. Higher margins and a decline in losses could lead to a rapid improvement in market sentiment and deliver good returns in the stock, making it a Buy at the current price.

Investor Takeaway

NIO stock has seen a 30% dip after the recent earnings. The company missed both the EPS and revenue estimates. However, it was able to report over 45% YoY growth in deliveries and a 420 basis points YoY improvement in gross margin. NIO has over 40% market share in the premium BEV segment in China, where it competes with Tesla. We could see a greater customer preference towards domestic brands due to the current geopolitical tensions, which can lower the competitive pressure on NIO and improve its margins.

A launch in the lucrative EU market will also be a tailwind for the stock. We could see a better trading arrangement between China and the EU with lower tariffs, as both deal with higher tariffs from the US. Lower tariffs could improve NIO’s performance in the EU and boost the margins rapidly.

data.title: Upslope Capital's Q1 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Fellow Investor,

Upslope’s objective is to deliver attractive, equity-like returns with significantly reduced market risk and low correlation versus traditional equity strategies. Q1 was challenging. However, in the two weeks since Mar 31, the world has changed, owing to an intense realignment of global trade policies. This has swiftly aligned markets more with Upslope’s portfolio and approach. April-to-date performance1 has been strong (approx. +7%), leaving the Fund +2% YTD (vs. -12% for the S&P Midcap 400).

Note: LPs/clients should always check individual statements for returns, which may vary due to timing, fee schedules and other factors. Since inception returns, downside standard deviation, and Sortino are all annualized figures.

MARKET CONDITIONS – A CRAZY GAME OF POKER

I don’t know how the global trade war will shake out, but I do think that recent events will: (1) dampen valuation multiples in the U.S. (already high on an absolute basis and relative to the rest of the world) and

(2) put the outlook for earnings at great risk. Why?

  • Investors around the world have discovered that a small group of individuals in the U.S. has the ability to rapidly implement major policy changes that materially impact businesses, trading partners, and allies. Regardless of whether one views these changes as good or bad, this means less predictability and higher volatility – factors that necessarily lead to lower valuation multiples.
  • Consumer and corporate confidence have taken an enormous hit year-to-date. “Weaker than during the 2008 Financial Crisis,” as Apollo’s Torsten Sløk recently put it. Why confidence is down isn’t very important. What matters is that without a rapid U-turn, it bodes poorly for earnings.

Given the above, Upslope’s portfolio continues to skew towards overseas investments, which should benefit from better valuations, a continued reversal of 15+ years of investor flows, and, an improving fiscal outlook. While Q1 had already been busy in terms of portfolio adjustments, activity accelerated in April as the crisis unfolded. This is logical and similar to past crises: when equities move as much in a single day as they often do in a full year, opportunities and mistakes bubble up much faster, and action is warranted.

PORTFOLIO POSITIONING

As of this writing, gross and beta-adjusted net exposures were 146% and 49%, respectively. Positioning reflects an above average number of perceived opportunities on the long side of the portfolio.

PORTFOLIO UPDATES

The largest contributors to and detractors from quarterly performance are noted below. Gross contribution to overall portfolio return is noted in parentheses.

Key Longs Exited

AptarGroup (ATR, specialty pharmaceutical and food/bev packaging business) – the Fund exited Aptar after owning shares nearly since inception. This wasn’t a hard thesis break, but I sold due to continued management malaise and significantly better opportunities elsewhere due to recent market dislocations.

Charles River (CRL, testing, discovery, and safety for biopharma) – sold due to a thesis break when the FDA announced the phase-out of animal testing requirements for certain drugs. This was a risk I’d worried about, and given an already-challenging environment for the company I decided the thesis (cyclical bottom) had been broken.

VF Corp (VFC, parent company of North Face, Vans, and other brands) – I began selling VF in Q1 because as markets corrected it became clear that other more straight-forward investment opportunities were emerging (vs. VF’s somewhat high-wire turnaround). The announcement of potentially aggressive tariffs in early April solidified this view.

Barry Callebaut (BARN-SW (OTCPK:BYCBF)(OTCPK:BRRLY), leading outsourced chocolate producer) – finished exiting Barry Callebaut early in Q1 on a thesis (timing cocoa peak) break. The Fund continues to own shares of Hershey, as the durability of its brands should give it greater staying power.

Current Long Portfolio Discussion

Given how fluid markets are, a discussion of the Fund’s overall long portfolio and its construction seems like a better use of time and space than a detailed review of individual holdings. I plan to elaborate further on some of the Fund’s new positions in upcoming quarterly letters and/or monthly Fact Sheets (note: the latter is distributed to current LPs and prospective investors – contact me if you would like to receive these and are not currently). For now, a brief summary of a few new positions is included below.

Upslope’s long portfolio can be divided into four distinct categories. This bucketing is partly deliberate (top-down search) and partly by accident (landed on similar bottom-up opportunities). Sizing is roughly equal among the categories, which are (details on each further below):

  • Re-arm and Re-shore
  • European Resurgence
  • Long Vol(atility)
  • Out-of-Favor Defensives Re-arm and Re-shore

This has been an unofficial, if deliberate, theme in the portfolio since early 2022, around the outset of the war in Ukraine. The war, in conjunction with the pandemic, I believe created lasting global change in terms of how countries view defense spending and supply chains. The Fund’s disclosed holdings include: Teledyne (TDY, specialty sensors and electronics for aerospace/defense and industrials – effectively spanning “re-arm and re-shore”), as well as the Fund’s European defense investments (Chemring (OTCPK:CMGMF)(OTCPK:CMGMY) and QinetiQ (OTCPK:QNTQF)(QNTQY)). During Q1 and in April, the Fund also added two Starter positions that should benefit from long-term reshoring.

European Resurgence

I’ve discussed the Fund’s outsized European exposure, which came about organically, for some time now. I effectively consider developments YTD – most notably German fiscal reform and the breakout of a global trade war – as confirmation of the “overweight” Europe thesis. In light of these changes, as well as attractive relative valuations and underweight investor positioning in Europe, I continued to add to the Fund’s European long exposure in Q1. In addition to the defense longs, the Fund also owns several ‘quality’ industrials of varying cyclicality:

  • Diploma (DPLM-LON)(OTCPK:DPMAY)(OTC:DPLMF) – specialty distributor focused on essential consumables across life sciences, seals (machinery), and controls (wiring and other) – owned since 2020.
  • Spirax (SPX-LON)(OTCPK:SPXSF)(OTCPK:SPXSY) – provider of steam systems, pumps, and electric thermal solutions for industrial and biopharma applications.
  • Holcim (HOLN-SWX)(OTCPK:HCMLF)(OTCPK:HCMLY) – global aggregates, cement and building materials supplier with deep expertise in emerging markets and a pending spinoff of US operations.
  • Sandvik (SAND-SE)(OTCPK:SDVKF)(OTCPK:SDVKY) – Sweden-based provider of mining, metal-cutting, and other industrial equipment and tools with outsized exposure to precious metals miners.

Each of the above companies has at least one of the following attributes that I believe will help partially blunt the impact of a potential trade war: essential products at a low cost, monopoly-like offerings, localized production/sales, and superior technology.

Long Vol(atility)

Given the geopolitical climate, I believe volatility will be elevated for several years (although clearly not at the current extreme level). Given this, volume-driven financial exchanges and other businesses that benefit from macro volatility should continue to thrive. The Fund’s disclosed holdings include: CME Group (CME) and Japan Exchange (OTCPK:JPXGY)(OTCPK:OSCUF).

In Q1, the Fund also added MarketAxess (MKTX, a leading credit and fixed income exchange), a business Upslope has owned (and at times shorted) several times over the years. MKTX has had a challenging five years or so, but (1) should see stabilized/improved market share with higher volatility, (2) trades at a deep enough discount to consider the possibility of a take-out in the near-to-medium term.

The Fund also added a Starter in a business services company that benefits from elevated macro volatility.

Out-of-Favor Defensives

Typically, the rising prospect of a recession leads to sharp outperformance of defensively cyclical businesses (e.g. consumer staples). And while that has indeed been the case, for a variety of reasons the market has also seen dispersion in the group, with a larger than typical (from my observation) number left behind. The Fund’s disclosed holdings include: Hershey and DSM Firmenich. The Fund also added three Starters, including shares of a company I’ve followed for a decade trading near a decade-low valuation, despite strong execution. The other two are healthcare related.

CLOSING THOUGHTS

Much has changed since the last letter: a lot of exuberance has receded, defensives have begun to outperform, and international (ex-US) equities have come into relative favor. Given how long defensives and international stocks were out of favor – the latter backed by 15+ years of investor flows – it’s hard to think these trends will revert back soon, even if a near-term pause might be due.

As always, thank you for the trust you’ve placed in me and Upslope to manage a portion of your hard- earned money. Please contact me if you have any questions at all, would like to add to your investment, or know a qualified investor who may be a good fit for Upslope’s unique approach.

Sincerely,

George K. Livadas

1-720-465-7033 | george@upslopecapital.com

Footnotes

1 Estimates are net of fees for Class A interest and as of April 11, 2025 close.

2 Returns prior to June 2023 are for a composite of all separate accounts invested according to Upslope’s long/short strategy. Subsequent returns are for Class A interest in Upslope Partners Fund, LP. See important performance-related details and disclosures in Appendix A.

3 Beta-/delta-adjusted.

4 Calculated as: (Net return since inception - 2.0% risk-free rate) / downside deviation.

5 Previously a “Starter” position that has now been exited.
