data.title: Tan Brings The Knife, Intel Stock Will Bring The Gains: Reiterating Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last time I covered Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC), my rating was a buy based on their new CEO Li-Bu Tan being a lot tougher on trimming the fat. I previously stated that Tan would make significant changes at Intel, and it seems that's precisely what he has done. On Monday, Intel announced it would be selling half its stake in Altera to Silver Lake, a move that frees up quite a bit of capital for Intel and also offloads a significant weight on their shoulders in terms of workforce.

I once said that Intel wasn’t a stock for the long game, and I still remain true to that. The stock is down 21% this past month among all the news about tariffs, and jumping in at current levels around $20 per share or below could prove the short-term turnaround stock investors are hoping for.

I believe that Tan isn't finished yet in terms of efficiency changes, with further job cuts still to come. Intel is expected to report earnings very soon, in just over a week (April 24th), with the changes Tan has already made, I expect Intel to beat earnings estimates of $12.31 billion in next quarter earnings, which is already on the lower end of previous expectations. I’m still buy-rated on Intel, and current selloffs due to newly announced tariffs only open up more entry points for investors looking for a near-term gain.

Intel Trimming the Fat

Intel stock is currently trading around $20.30, up 3.4% since Monday on the news of the Altera sale. For those investors that don't know, Altera was a daughter organization of Intel focused on producing programmable chips. Intel is selling a majority stake in the company, so 51% will go to Silver Lake for a price tag of $4.5 billion. This values Altera at $8.75 billion.

If you ask me, this is a huge weight off Intel's shoulders and a wise move by Tan. Since Intel acquired Altera in 2015, it has significantly underperformed, only contributing to 3% of Intel’s revenue in 2024 and posting an operating loss of $615 million. On top of this, Altera has roughly 3,000 employees, so Intel offloading over half of those paychecks trims the fat further.

Tan has always remained focused on shifting Intel back to what it’s good. Competitors like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are tipped to be making a deal with TSMC (TSM) which will allow them to manufacture chips at their foundry in Arizona, giving them some reprise from any upcoming tariffs. I expect the cash from Intel's latest deal with Silver Lake to give them so breathing room, especially to help reverse their steep operating loss of over $13 billion reported in 2024. I’m expecting Tan to experiment with more cost-cutting measures, and think this pocketed $4.5 billion could better position him to do just that. Year-to-date intel is up 1.24%, which is a much more attractive number than the S&P 500's (SP500) 7.74% decline over the same period.

Intel is expected to report earnings on the 24th of this month. Wall Street puts guidance at $12.31 billion, which is in line with Intel's guidance back in January of $11.7-12.7 billion.

So, can investors expect Tan's cost-cutting efficiency to show up in this upcoming report earnings? I believe they can, and here’s why. Within the current 90-day tariff pause, I expect the trading war to end, and Intel will be totally tariff-free. To back this up, news came over the weekend that semiconductors would be free from reciprocal tariffs. This foreshadows the long-term future for chip tariffs, and their effects should be negligible on Intel. Along with this, Microsoft Windows 10 support is expected to end in October, and pre-orders for PCs should show up in the next report earning guidance; this makes me believe that Intel should guide much higher for Q3, reflecting increased demand for their computer client business.

A More Precise Foundary Business

Intel's foundry business has for quite some time underperformed; the business reported operating losses of $2.3 billion and $5.8 billion in 4Q24 and 3Q24, respectively, as you can see in the chart below. Investors can expect Tan to get these numbers under control in 2025, as the excessive spending under former CEO Pat Gelsinger is something of the past. Although Intel buys its semiconductors from TSMC, they are still one of the only U.S.-based foundries businesses in the U.S., and among the only ones when it comes to advanced chips. President Trump's promise to bring manufacturing back to the U.S. should favor Intel. In fact, we may even see Intel’s foundry business as its own wholly owned subsidiary sometime soon, as suggested by the VP of investor relations John Pitzer.

Valuation

Intel stock, for me, is grossly undervalued. The stock is down 44% over the last year, making it very attractive to investors. If we look across the industry, Intel's forward EV-to-sales ratio is far lower than industry competitors like AMD, Qualcomm (QCOM), and TSMC. The same trend follows in the forward price-to-sales ratio.

The graph I made below to compare ratios highlights Intel’s extremely low valuation. The market cap of Intel is just under $89 billion; they’re sitting just above their 52-week low of $17.66. The stock right now is undervalued, but investors shouldn’t view Intel as a growth stock but rather a value pick. I expect Intel to recover to stock prices of the low $30s after earnings at the end of the month.

What's Next?

Li-Bu Tan is on an efficacy run, and he doesn’t seem afraid of committing to big changes at Intel after dumping half of Altera. He definitely wants to line up with a more streamlined core business and give Intel a direct track to follow in 2025. I’m watching to see what happens with Intel’s foundry business when it comes to the joint venture with TSMC which was discussed earlier this month but got overshadowed by all the tariff panic. I also think we’ll see Tan announce further job cuts in 2025, something that might come up, in my opinion, on the company’s earnings call later this month.

I expect much healthier guidance for 2Q25, as they now have a decent amount of capital freed up to experiment with more cost-saving tactics and head into what should be a growth period for PCs. I remain buy-rated on Intel and look for a sub $20 per share entry position into the stock. I last wrote on Intel when it was trading at $22.38, and now 9% lower, I think the stock is attractively trading at a discount.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Trade War And Inflation Make Quick Service Fast Food Industry More Enticing
data.description:
With the ongoing trade war likely to push inflation ever higher, Americans, among others, will likely begin tightening their belts, which will put pressure on many consumer discretionary companies. However, I believe that quick service fast food restaurants, such as McDonald's (MCD) and Wendy's (WEN), actually stand to benefit as consumers continue to dine out while also looking to reduce spending on food outside of the home. In other words, they'll be looking for cheaper options. Meanwhile, pricier restaurants, including higher-end fast casual restaurants like Chipotle (CMG), will have to contend with downward pressure and more price-conscious consumers.

My thesis essentially breaks down to:

  • High inflation and economic uncertainty will encourage consumers to tighten their belts. Value-oriented industries, like fast food, may actually benefit from price-conscious consumers. Quite simply, fast food offers one of the cheaper and most convenient ways to get calories.
  • The United States economy is still in solid shape, and being less dependent on trade than many other countries, should have an easier time coping with near-term headwinds created by the trade war. That said, if the trade war worsens or continues for a prolonged period, it could drag the American economy down, potentially into a contraction. Likewise, economic contractions in other major economies will put pressure on the United States.
  • Regardless, fast food restaurants fared quite well during the Great Recession and may be a safe haven. The past doesn't guarantee the future, but fast food may prove more resilient (once again due to low prices) than other industries even if the economy should start to contract.
  • Most fast food ingredients are sourced locally, or at least nationally, which will, to some extent, shield quick service restaurants from the inflationary pressures of the trade war. Of course, even nationally sourced ingredients will be impacted by the trade war, and if prices for commodities like beef rise dramatically globally, it'll put upward pressure on domestically sourced ingredients.

At the end of this analysis, I will briefly examine a few quick service fast food stocks. These brief overviews are simply first impressions, but I do plan to take a deeper dive into individual quick service fast food stocks in the near future.

Regarding "safe havens," I must note that I am primarily talking about relatively. Any severe contractions or prolonged slowdowns will have a detrimental impact on essentially all companies. Even then, as far as market conditions are concerned, I am confident that the fast food sector will prove to be among the more resilient. However, many fast food companies are carrying high debt burdens, making their financial footing more tenuous.

More Affordable Prices Will Make Quick Service Fast Food a More Appetizing Choice

The ongoing trade war is likely to kick inflation into high gear, although the 90-day pause on "reciprocal" tariffs should ease inflationary pressures. However, with China still being hit with 145% tariffs, expect prices for many goods to rise nonetheless. Further, the 10% general tariff on most countries will push prices up, and even if all tariffs were dropped, inflation could continue apace.

The basic relationship between price and demand is well-known: Lower prices generally equate to increased demand, while higher prices dampen demand. With tariffs now in effect, we can expect the prices for many products to rise substantially, which should cool demand for many goods. Yet not all products and services will see prices increase at the same pace.

Admittedly, quick service fast food prices have risen in recent years, expanding by 60% between 2014 and 2024. Prices will likely rise in the future, but I believe the increase will not be as dramatic as it will be for other products, like automobiles and electronics. Demand for cars and electronics, among other things, will likely cool off. Yet, consumers will still consume and have money to spend. If fast food prices don't rise as quickly as prices for other goods, demand could shift to fast food, thus spurring sales.

Further, higher-priced items at fast food restaurants, like a Big Mac, could take an especially hard hit if inflation kicks up or the economy contracts. Value meals, like McDonald's $5 Meal Deal or Wendy's Biggie Bag should still offer enough value to attract customers. (This isn't to say that value meals are immune to price increases, but quick service restaurants will still offer as much value as they can.)

The shift from higher-priced and higher-margin items to value menu items will negatively impact fast food restaurants, but continued high overall demand will soften the blow.

Americans Love Spending on Food and Especially Take Out

In 2023, the average American consumer unit spent $9,985 on food, with $6,053 spent on food at home and $3,933 spent on food away from home. This is up from $5,259 and $3,030, respectively, in 2021. Spending on food away from home jumped 20% in 21-22 and 8.1% in 22-23.

One study found that the average American eats out three times a month and orders delivery or takeout about 4.5 times a month. Another survey found that the average American eats out 5 to 6 times a week.

Food Spending Habits During the Great Recession

You'll find tons of studies measuring spending habits during the Great Recession, and to no surprise, consumers tightened their belts, and spending on food, in particular, decreased, although calories consumed did not decline as steeply. Thus, people continued eating quite a bit, but strove to cut spending. Ultimately, between 2006 and 2009, consumers reduced food spending by 5%, according to data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS).

Consumers have become very price conscious. One way consumers tightened their belts was to spend more time hunting for deals and using coupons. In a sense, many people laid off, forced into lower-paying jobs, or who saw their hours cut, shifted from paid work to non-paid work (bargain hunting).

As seen in the chart above, food-away-from-home took the biggest hit. This includes fast food and more formal sit-down restaurants. Still, as I'll outline below, data suggests that while consumers ate out less often overall during the Great Recession, spending on fast food remained high, and in some cases, quick service chains enjoyed rising net income and revenues.

Fast Food Did Well During the Great Recession

I've seen articles in recent weeks arguing that an economic downturn would batter fast food as customers tighten their belts. Yet, that premise didn't strike me as immediately true for a few reasons. For one, fast food actually offers one of the cheaper ways to get calories.

The massive buying power of a company like McDonald's or Wendy's, in combination with easy-to-cook food and the ability to automate various aspects of operations, such as taking orders, allows fast food companies to offer affordable menu items. Eating food at home will typically be cheaper than fast food, but fast food remains an affordable option, especially when you factor in the time it takes to shop and cook.

So even when consumers batten down the financial hatches, that doesn't automatically mean quick service is off the menu. At least, I theorize this could be true. After all, you can get a $5 Biggie Bag from Wendy's or a $5 Meal Deal from McDonald's. Meanwhile, even the cheapest frozen pizzas in the grocery store often cost more than $5. By the time you're done buying ingredients to make a deli sandwich, let alone a burger, you're going to be spending a few bucks even with the cheapest stuff you grab on sale.

Data supports my argument that fast food did well during the Great Recession. Below you'll see that Yum! (YUM) enjoyed steady annual net income growth even amid the Great Recession. McDonald's took a short-term hit but quickly recovered. Wendy's only managed to maintain, but amid a major recession when other companies were dealing with slumping demand, treading water is still a good achievement.

[object HTMLElement]

Revenues for YUM, WEN, and MCD paint a similar picture:

[object HTMLElement]

Yet while these three fast food companies performed well in terms of revenues and net income, stock prices did take a hit. YUM's upward march was slowed, while MCD flatlined then declined. WEN suffered a steady drop. This was quite likely fueled by overall market sentiments and general cynicism surrounding the economy. If anything, the declining share prices for WEN, MCD, and YUM while underlying fundamentals (e.g. net income) remained solid-to-strong made them a more attractive buy. The same could prove true in a future recession, as well as during the trade war.

[object HTMLElement]

Fast Food is Global but America Remains King

For YUM, MCD, and WEN, the American market remains the key market. MCD and YUM both have a large presence outside of the United States. McDonald's has about 15,000 stores in the United States, along with 5,000 in Japan, 2,200 in France, 1,436 in the UK, and 1,500 in Germany. Even tiny Singapore has a whopping 151 locations. WEN isn't as exposed to international markets, but the company does plan to expand its global footprint, eying new markets as a key opportunity for growth.

Tariffs Could Actually Benefit Value-Oriented Fast Food Chains

For American quick service fast food restaurants, the American market is crucial. If American consumers come under pressure owing to inflation, and particularly inflation for products like electronics, spending habits may shift away from pricey foreign goods to local products and services, including fast food. A family might opt against upgrading their TV for now, but that could provide more money to spend at McDonald's, Disney World, local movie theaters, and other localized businesses.

Fast food restaurants source most of their ingredients on a more localized level. In the United States, McDonald's gets their beef primarily from Lopez Foods in Oklahoma City and Keystone Foods in Pennsylvania. Wendy's "fresh never frozen" mantra, meanwhile, makes sourcing locally especially important. In overseas markets too, quick service restaurants often get ingredients locally, so even if other countries start to raise tariffs, fast food giants may be at least partially insulated.

That said, tariffs could still have an impact. For example, the United States gets much of its potash, which is crucial for growing food and feed crops, from Canada. Potash is subjected to 10% (for non-CUSMA exempted). Potash prices have been on the rise already, and tariffs could push the price tag even higher.

Potash is only one of many examples of how tariffs could push up costs for fast food restaurants. Still, compared to automobile manufacturing and various other industries, the food industry remains more localized and less vulnerable.

Prices for beef, chicken, and other foodstuffs have been on the rise. Not only could tariffs affect even a relatively localized agriculture industry, but deporting immigrants could leave meat-processing factories and agricultural operations short-staffed. Wages could rise substantially, which is great for the workers, but will also contribute to higher prices for society as a whole.

A Quick Peek at Quick Service Fast Food Stocks and Quant Ratings

I've only just begun my analysis on individual fast food companies and am quite far from drawing any concrete conclusions. However, I do have some initial impressions to share.

McDonald's

Seeking Alpha's quant tools currently give MCD an F rating in terms of value. MCD scores poorly in Price / Sales (TTM) at $8.76 and the P/E GAAP (TTM) leaves a lot to desire at ~27. YOY revenue growth was 1.67%, below the 2.4% sector median, and growth is a major concern for me. McDonald's is already so mature of a company, with so many locations not just in the United States but also globally, that finding new areas to grow could prove especially challenging.

The quant ratings also give MCD a C rating for YOY revenue growth, which I'd say is rather generous. At a quick glance, I'd put MCD as a hold. Seeking Alpha's quant ratings agree.

Wendy's

Wendy's does a bit better, with the Seeking Alpha quant tools giving the company a solid B for valuation. The P/E GAAP (TTM) weighs in at an enticing ~14.00, below the 16.68 sector median. WEN gets a D- for growth, with their working capital shrinking by 25.72% and revenue growth (YoY) amounting to just 2.98%. It should be noted, however, that Wendy's is preparing to make a strong international push, which will generate risks but could also pave the way for higher growth.

The quant scores give WEN a B in valuation, although I admit that with Wendy's high debt levels, I do think that is rather generous. WEN's growth rating is a D-, in large part due to a dividend cut. However, Wendy's move to rein in the dividend I believe will improve the company's financial situation. Seeking Alpha's quant ratings give WEN a hold, which I believe is fair, but at a quick glance, I think there's a fair chance Wendy's will end up being a "buy" for me.

Yum!

Revenue YOY is up 6.68%, which is certainly encouraging, but the P/E GAAP (TTM) is quite high at ~28. Taco Bell is one of the crown jewels in Yum!'s portfolio and has been performing particularly well. Price/Sales (TTM) is also rather high at ~5.5. YUM is a hold according to the quant tools, but may end up a buy for me.

Considering Fast Casual Restaurants

I believe fast casual restaurants may feel the pressure of tightening consumer wallets, and thus I'm a bit wary of investing in them, at least for now. Further research could change my mind. The best fast casual restaurant chains have put in strong performances in recent years, and some of them have the potential for a lot of growth. Still, some chains are facing headwinds that make me especially nervous amid the trade war.

Chipotle's TTM P/E of ~41 would be easy to stomach if the brand was still rapidly expanding. Revenue (YOY, fiscal year ending 12/31/24) growth has been strong but may be leveling off, coming in at a respectable but not inspiring 13%, reaching $11.31 billion, and Chipotle already has $4.54 billion in debt. The risk of rising ingredient costs and already somewhat narrow profit margin at 13.56% could create pressure. Net income growth has been slow but steady in recent years. I wouldn't rule out investing in Chipotle, but with the potential for industry headwinds to kick up, I'm not rushing to invest in them either.

[object HTMLElement]

[object HTMLElement]

One fast casual stock that looks rather appealing at first glance is Wingstop (NYSE: WING), which in the 2024 fiscal year enjoyed YOY annual revenue growth of over 27%, along with a profit margin of 17.37% and an operating margin of 25.82%. Debit is a bit high at $1.27 billion, with revenues coming in at $625.81 million. The company has $315.91 million in cash. All in all, the high profit and operating margin suggest that Wingstop has a bit of room to cut prices if needed, and enough cash to sustain a crunch, although perhaps not a prolonged contraction. The sharp decline in WING's price does make it an interesting prospect.

Quick Take-Away: Quick Service Fast Food is an Industry to Watch

I haven't narrowed down which stocks, if any, to invest in, but I do believe that the quick service fast food industry offers some of the most appetizing prospects. I find the performance of McDonald's, Yum!, and Wendy's during the Great Recession to be especially promising, and I believe that even if a recession were to strike or the trade war drags on and slows growth, quick service restaurants can still do well.
